title: Hash-Chained audit_chain Table with Append-Only Triggers

story.as_a: tenant operator needing tamper-evident audit of every custody-critical event in my tenant
story.i_want_to: have a hash-chained, append-only database table where every entry references the previous entry's hash, every insert is validated by a trigger, and the production database role cannot disable the triggers or delete rows
story.so_that: retroactive tampering of the audit log requires compromising the database at a level that leaves separate evidence, and routine application bugs cannot silently rewrite history

rationale: The per-tenant audit chain is the foundation of Layer 2 tamper evidence in the chain-of-custody v2 stack. Without a hash-chained append-only store enforced by database triggers, every other layer collapses to advisory checks. This story ships the schema and the enforcement — STH computation (US-26.1.2) and agent-witnessed verification (Phase 5) build on top. The design pattern (hash chain + triggers + position monotonicity) is well-understood from blockchain and Certificate Transparency literature and maps cleanly to PostgreSQL's trigger system.

acceptance_criteria.description: Migration creates `audit_chain` table with columns: id (uuid PK), tenant_id (uuid FK NOT NULL), chain_position (bigint NOT NULL), prev_entry_hash (bytea NOT NULL, 32 bytes), action (text NOT NULL), actor_lineage (jsonb NOT NULL, list of dispatch IDs), entity_type (text NOT NULL), entity_id (uuid), payload (jsonb NOT NULL), inserted_at (timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()), entry_hash (bytea NOT NULL, 32 bytes).

Unique constraint on `(tenant_id, chain_position)` ensures no gaps or duplicates in any tenant's chain. Index on `(tenant_id, inserted_at DESC)` for timeline queries. Index on `(tenant_id, action)` for filtering by action type.
BEFORE UPDATE trigger `audit_chain_prevent_update` raises a `cannot_modify_audit_chain` exception on any attempt to update any column of any row. The trigger fires for ALL updates, no exceptions.
BEFORE DELETE trigger `audit_chain_prevent_delete` raises a `cannot_delete_audit_chain` exception on any attempt to delete any row. The trigger fires for ALL deletes, no exceptions.

BEFORE INSERT trigger `audit_chain_verify_invariants` validates three conditions: (a) `chain_position` equals `(SELECT MAX(chain_position) FROM audit_chain WHERE tenant_id = NEW.tenant_id) + 1`, OR 0 if no prior entries; (b) `prev_entry_hash` equals the previous entry's `entry_hash` for this tenant, or 32 zero bytes if this is the genesis entry; (c) `entry_hash` equals the SHA-256 of `tenant_id || chain_position || prev_entry_hash || canonical_json_of(action, actor_lineage, entity_type, entity_id, payload) || inserted_at`.

The production database role (e.g., `loopctl_prod`) lacks DDL privileges (`NOCREATE`, `NOSUPERUSER`, cannot ALTER TABLE). Triggers cannot be dropped or disabled from within the application. Documented in deployment notes with a sample `GRANT` / `REVOKE` script.

`Loopctl.AuditChain.append/2` context function is the only public API for writing entries. Signature: `append(tenant_id, %{action, actor_lineage, entity_type, entity_id, payload})`. The function computes chain_position, prev_entry_hash, and entry_hash inside an Ecto.Multi with SELECT...FOR UPDATE on the previous entry to prevent concurrent insert races.

`Loopctl.AuditChain.list_entries/2` reads entries for a tenant with standard pagination. It is tenant-scoped via RLS policy: `USING (tenant_id = current_tenant_id())`. AdminRepo bypasses RLS for superadmin queries.

Genesis entry for a tenant (the first `tenant_created` event) has chain_position = 0, prev_entry_hash = 32 zero bytes. This is written as part of the signup transaction (dependency on US-26.0.1, US-26.0.2).
Tenant isolation test: tenant A cannot read, write to, or see tenant B's audit_chain entries. Confirmed via RLS policy tests with both Repo (RLS-enforced) and AdminRepo (BYPASSRLS) paths.

Concurrent append test: two processes attempt to append to the same tenant's chain simultaneously. Both operations either succeed with sequential positions (one gets N, one gets N+1) or one fails gracefully — no case where both succeed with the same position, no case where the chain has a gap.
OpenAPI spec updates: new `AuditChainEntry` schema. Operation specs for any new read endpoints (if added in this story or deferred to a later docs story). CHANGELOG entry describing the new table.

test_cases.name: Append genesis entry succeeds with zero prev_entry_hash
Second entry references first entry's hash
UPDATE trigger blocks any modification
steps: Attempt raw Repo.update_all to change a column
expected_results: Database raises exception with message mentioning cannot_modify_audit_chain

test_cases.name: DELETE trigger blocks any row removal
INSERT with wrong prev_entry_hash is rejected
steps: Attempt a raw insert with prev_entry_hash = 0x42 × 32

test_cases.name: INSERT with wrong chain_position is rejected
Tenant isolation: cross-tenant reads return nothing
Concurrent appends produce sequential positions, no gaps
steps: Spawn 10 concurrent tasks each calling append/2 with distinct payloads
expected_results: chain_position values are 0..9 with no gaps or duplicates
Each prev_entry_hash matches the previous entry_hash

technical_notes: Trigger functions are PL/pgSQL. Use `EXECUTE execute_error(...)` to raise with a stable error code.

The entry_hash computation must be canonical — use Jason.encode! with sorted keys, OR compute the hash in application code and store it (the trigger verifies it matches). Application-computed is simpler; the trigger just verifies.
SELECT...FOR UPDATE in append/2 locks the previous entry row to serialize concurrent appends. Without this lock, two callers can both read position N and both try to insert at N+1.
RLS policy: ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY (not FORCE — the production schema_admin is owner) with `USING (tenant_id = current_tenant_id())`.
Migration file for the trigger functions should use `execute/2` with multi-line strings for the PL/pgSQL bodies.
AdminRepo is used by the STH computation (US-26.1.2) which needs to scan all tenants. Regular Repo is used for append.
